<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Data model definitions
// ============================================

class TestMultipleObserver : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    TestMultipleObserver(const std::string& name) : m_name(name), m_changed(false) {}

    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_changed = changed;
        m_callCount++;
    }

    bool wasChanged() const { return m_changed; }
    int getCallCount() const { return m_callCount; }
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }

    void reset() {
        m_changed = false;
        m_callCount = 0;
    }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    bool m_changed;
    int m_callCount = 0;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test multiple observers watching a single target
 *
 * @param targetName Name for the target node
 * @param observerNames Vector of names for observer nodes
 * @return std::pair<int, bool> Pair of total call count and whether all observers were notified
 */
std::pair<int, bool> test_multiple_observers(const std::string& targetName, const std::vector<std::string>& observerNames) {
    // 1. Create target node
    auto target = std::make_shared<TestMultipleObserver>(targetName);
    
    // 2. Create observer nodes
    std::vector<std::shared_ptr<TestMultipleObserver>> observers;
    observers.reserve(observerNames.size());
    
    for (const auto& name : observerNames) {
        observers.push_back(std::make_shared<TestMultipleObserver>(name));
    }
    
    // 3. Get observer graph instance
    auto& observerGraph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // 4. Add all observers to watch target
    for (const auto& observer : observers) {
        observerGraph.addObserver(observer, target);
    }
    
    // 5. Notify target
    target->notify();
    
    // 6. Count notifications and verify all observers were notified
    int totalCallCount = 0;
    bool allNotified = true;
    
    for (const auto& observer : observers) {
        totalCallCount += observer->getCallCount();
        if (observer->getCallCount() == 0) {
            allNotified = false;
        }
    }
    
    // 7. Return total call count and notification status
    return std::make_pair(totalCallCount, allNotified);
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <tuple>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test batch execution reduces recomputation count
 *
 * @param initialA Initial value for variable a
 * @param initialB Initial value for variable b
 * @param nonBatchA Value to set a without batching
 * @param nonBatchB Value to set b without batching
 * @param batchA Value to set a within batch
 * @param batchB Value to set b within batch
 * @return std::tuple<int, int, int> {final sum, count after non-batch updates, count after batch updates}
 */
std::tuple<int, int, int> test_batch_execution(int initialA,
                                               int initialB,
                                               int nonBatchA,
                                               int nonBatchB,
                                               int batchA,
                                               int batchB) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto a = reaction::var(initialA);
    auto b = reaction::var(initialB);
    
    // Create variable to track recomputation count
    auto count = reaction::var(0);
    
    // Create calculation that increments count and returns sum
    auto sum = reaction::calc([&]() {
        count.value(count() + 1);
        return a() + b();
    });
    
    // Perform non-batch updates
    a.value(nonBatchA);
    b.value(nonBatchB);
    
    // Record recompute count after non-batch updates
    int countAfterNonBatch = count();
    
    // Create batch context and execute batch
    reaction::batch([&]() {
        a.value(batchA);
        b.value(batchB);
    }).execute();
    
    // Record recompute count after batch updates
    int countAfterBatch = count();
    
    // Return final sum and counts
    return std::make_tuple(sum(), countAfterNonBatch, countAfterBatch);
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <tuple>
#include <cmath>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test filter trigger with conditional recomputation
 *
 * @param initialValue Initial sensor value
 * @param firstUpdate First update (expected to be within threshold)
 * @param secondUpdate Second update (expected to exceed threshold)
 * @param diffThreshold Minimum delta to trigger recomputation
 * @param levelThreshold Threshold to categorize HIGH vs NORMAL
 * @return std::tuple<std::string, int, int>
 *         {final status string, recompute count after first update, recompute count after second update}
 */
std::tuple<std::string, int, int> test_filter_trigger(double initialValue,
                                                      double firstUpdate,
                                                      double secondUpdate,
                                                      double diffThreshold,
                                                      double levelThreshold) {
    using namespace reaction;
    
    // 1. Create reactive variable for sensor
    auto sensor = var(initialValue);
    
    // 2. Create variables to track recomputation count and last processed value
    auto recomputeCount = var(0);
    auto lastProcessed = var(initialValue);
    
    // 3. Create filter-triggered calculation
    auto filteredCalc = calc([&]() {
        // This calculation will only recompute when the filter condition is met
        recomputeCount.value(recomputeCount.get() + 1);
        lastProcessed.value(sensor.get());
        return sensor.get(); // Return current sensor value
    });
    
    // 4. Apply filter: only trigger when absolute difference exceeds threshold
    filteredCalc.filter([](double current, double previous) {
        return std::abs(current - previous) > diffThreshold;
    }, sensor, lastProcessed);
    
    // 5. Update sensor with first value
    sensor.value(firstUpdate);
    
    // 6. Record recompute count after first update
    int countAfterFirst = recomputeCount.get();
    
    // 7. Update sensor with second value
    sensor.value(secondUpdate);
    
    // 8. Record recompute count after second update
    int countAfterSecond = recomputeCount.get();
    
    // 9. Get final status based on level threshold
    std::string finalStatus = (sensor.get() > levelThreshold) ? "HIGH" : "NORMAL";
    
    // 10. Return final status and counts
    return std::make_tuple(finalStatus, countAfterFirst, countAfterSecond);
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct PointerAccessSnapshot {
    int valueAfterFirstAdd;
    int valueAfterSecondAdd;
    bool pointerStable;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate pointer-style access for reactive structs.
 *
 * @param initialValue Initial value stored inside the Counter
 * @param delta Amount added on each pointer invocation
 * @return PointerAccessSnapshot Snapshot capturing results after pointer operations
 */
inline PointerAccessSnapshot test_react_pointer_access(int initialValue, int delta) {
    // Define Counter struct
    struct Counter {
        int value;
        Counter(int v = 0) : value(v) {}
        void add(int delta) { value += delta; }
    };
    
    // Create reactive variable with Counter
    auto counterVar = reaction::var(Counter(initialValue));
    
    // Get first pointer
    auto firstPtr = counterVar.getPtr();
    
    // Call add method through pointer
    firstPtr->add(delta);
    
    // Get current value after first add
    int valueAfterFirstAdd = counterVar.get();
    
    // Get second pointer
    auto secondPtr = counterVar.getPtr();
    
    // Call add method through pointer again
    secondPtr->add(delta);
    
    // Get current value after second add
    int valueAfterSecondAdd = counterVar.get();
    
    // Check if pointer is stable
    bool pointerStable = (firstPtr == secondPtr);
    
    // Return pointer access snapshot
    return PointerAccessSnapshot{
        valueAfterFirstAdd,
        valueAfterSecondAdd,
        pointerStable
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

 3. Iterate over the set and collect the names (using `getName()`) into a vector.

 4. Check if the set size is 3 (meaning all names are unique) and set the boolean accordingly.

 5. Return the OrderingSnapshot.

 However, note: the function parameters are the names we want to assign to the reactive nodes. We are going to create three reactive variables and set their names to the given parameters.

 Code:

 Let's write the function accordingly.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <set>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace test_reaction {

struct OrderingSnapshot {
    std::vector<std::string> orderedNames;
    bool allUnique;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate React::operator< for ordering reactive handles.
 *
 * @param firstName Name assigned to the first reactive node
 * @param secondName Name assigned to the second reactive node
 * @param thirdName Name assigned to the third reactive node
 * @return OrderingSnapshot Snapshot of sorted names and uniqueness flag
 */
inline OrderingSnapshot test_react_ordering(const std::string &firstName,
                                            const std::string &secondName,
                                            const std::string &thirdName) {
    // Create three reactive variables with the provided names
    auto react1 = reaction::var(0).setName(firstName);
    auto react2 = reaction::var(0).setName(secondName);
    auto react3 = reaction::var(0).setName(thirdName);
    
    // Insert them into a set (which will order them using operator<)
    std::set<reaction::React<>> reactSet;
    reactSet.insert(react1);
    reactSet.insert(react2);
    reactSet.insert(react3);
    
    // Collect ordered names by iterating through the set
    std::vector<std::string> orderedNames;
    orderedNames.reserve(reactSet.size());
    for (const auto &react : reactSet) {
        orderedNames.push_back(react.getName());
    }
    
    // Check if all names are unique (set size should equal the number of elements)
    bool allUnique = (reactSet.size() == 3);
    
    return OrderingSnapshot{orderedNames, allUnique};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/memory/memory_config.h>
#include <reaction/memory/stack_monitor.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct StackAllocationSnapshot {
    bool smallAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool largeAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool runtimeAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool largeAllowedWithMonitoring;
    bool guardSucceeded;
    bool allocationBlockedAtLimit;
    bool runtimeAllowedAfterRelease;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate StackMonitor::canAllocate behaviour across modes.
 *
 * @param largeSize Value used for runtime canAllocate after release
 * @return StackAllocationSnapshot Snapshot of allocation checks before/after guard
 */
inline StackAllocationSnapshot test_stack_monitor_capacity(std::size_t largeSize) {
    StackAllocationSnapshot snapshot{};
    
    // 1. Disable monitoring
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::setMonitoring(false);
    
    // 2. Initialize stack monitor
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::initialize();
    
    // 3. Check allocation without monitoring (small size)
    snapshot.smallAllowedWithoutMonitoring = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::canAllocate(reaction::memory::SBOConfig::ALWAYS_SBO_SIZE);
    
    // 4. Check allocation without monitoring (large size)
    snapshot.largeAllowedWithoutMonitoring = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    // 5. Check allocation without monitoring (runtime size)
    snapshot.runtimeAllowedWithoutMonitoring = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    // 6. Enable monitoring
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::setMonitoring(true);
    
    // 7. Initialize stack monitor again
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::initialize();
    
    // 8. Check large allocation with monitoring
    snapshot.largeAllowedWithMonitoring = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    // 9. Create StackGuard in scope block
    {
        reaction::memory::StackGuard<largeSize> guard;
        
        // 10. Check if guard succeeded
        snapshot.guardSucceeded = guard.success();
        
        // 11. Check if allocation blocked at limit
        snapshot.allocationBlockedAtLimit = !guard.success();
    }
    
    // 12. Exit guard scope (automatic when block ends)
    
    // 13. Check runtime allocation after release
    snapshot.runtimeAllowedAfterRelease = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    return snapshot;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

**Note:** The implementation follows the exact sequence specified in the requirements. Key points:

1. Uses `reaction::memory::StackMonitor` functions as specified
2. Properly handles the template-based `StackGuard` with the `largeSize` parameter
3. Maintains the exact order of operations as specified
4. Correctly maps each step to the corresponding `StackAllocationSnapshot` field
5. Handles the scope block for `StackGuard` creation and automatic destruction

The function demonstrates the behavior of stack monitoring under different conditions (with/without monitoring) and shows how allocations succeed or fail based on the current stack usage limits.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/batch.h>
#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>
#include <memory>

namespace test_reaction {

struct DepthOrderingSnapshot {
    bool initialComparatorResult;
    bool shallowBeforeDeep;
    bool deepBeforeShallow;
    bool nullComesFirst;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate ObserverNode::updateDepth cooperating with BatchCompare ordering.
 *
 * @return DepthOrderingSnapshot Comparator outcomes after adjusting node depths.
 */
inline DepthOrderingSnapshot test_observer_depth_ordering() {
    // 1. Define DepthProbeNode class
    class DepthProbeNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
    public:
        void setDepth(uint16_t depth) {
            this->updateDepth(depth);
        }
    };
    
    // 2. Get thread manager instance
    auto& threadManager = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 3. Reset thread manager state
    threadManager.resetForTesting();
    
    // 4. Enable thread safety
    threadManager.enableThreadSafety();
    
    // 5. Create shallow and deep DepthProbeNode instances
    auto shallowNode = std::make_shared<DepthProbeNode>();
    auto deepNode = std::make_shared<DepthProbeNode>();
    
    // 6. Create NodeWeak from nodes
    auto shallowWeak = reaction::NodeWeak{shallowNode};
    auto deepWeak = reaction::NodeWeak{deepNode};
    
    // 7. Create BatchCompare instance
    reaction::BatchCompare batchCompare;
    
    // 8. Compare initial nodes (both have default depth 0)
    bool initialResult = batchCompare(shallowWeak, deepWeak);
    
    // 9. Update depths of nodes
    shallowNode->setDepth(1);
    deepNode->setDepth(2);
    
    // 10. Compare shallow before deep
    bool shallowBeforeDeepResult = batchCompare(shallowWeak, deepWeak);
    
    // 11. Update depth of shallow node again
    shallowNode->setDepth(3);
    
    // 12. Compare deep before shallow
    bool deepBeforeShallowResult = batchCompare(deepWeak, shallowWeak);
    
    // 13. Create null NodeWeak
    reaction::NodeWeak nullWeak;
    
    // 14. Compare null with shallow
    bool nullComesFirstResult = batchCompare(nullWeak, shallowWeak);
    
    // 15. Return depth ordering snapshot
    return DepthOrderingSnapshot{
        .initialComparatorResult = initialResult,
        .shallowBeforeDeep = shallowBeforeDeepResult,
        .deepBeforeShallow = deepBeforeShallowResult,
        .nullComesFirst = nullComesFirstResult
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

This implementation follows all the specified requirements:

1. **DepthProbeNode class**: Defined as a subclass of `ObserverNode` with a `setDepth` method
2. **Thread manager**: Gets the instance, resets state, and enables thread safety
3. **Nodes**: Creates shallow and deep nodes with shared pointers
4. **NodeWeak**: Creates weak references from the shared nodes
5. **BatchCompare**: Uses the functor to compare nodes
6. **Depth updates**: Sets different depths at various stages
7. **Comparison sequence**: Tests initial, shallow-before-deep, deep-before-shallow, and null comparisons
8. **Return value**: Returns a structured snapshot of all comparison results

The implementation correctly demonstrates how `ObserverNode::updateDepth` interacts with `BatchCompare` ordering based on depth values.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <reaction/expression/expression_builders.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct UnaryExpressionSnapshot {
    int initialNegatedSum;
    int updatedNegatedSum;
    bool logicalNotAfterFlip;
    int bitwiseNotValue;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate unary expression builders and operators on reactive values.
 *
 * This function showcases how low-level expression builder helpers (`make_binary_op`,
 * `make_unary_op`) and high-level unary operators (`-`, `!`, `~`) cooperate with the
 * `expr` factory to produce reactive expressions.
 *
 * @param a First input value participating in the sum expression
 * @param b Second input value participating in the sum expression
 * @return UnaryExpressionSnapshot Snapshot of expression outputs before/after updates
 */
inline UnaryExpressionSnapshot test_unary_expression_builders(int a, int b) {
    // 1. Create reactive variables
    auto var_a = reaction::var(a);
    auto var_b = reaction::var(b);
    auto var_flag = reaction::var(true);
    auto var_num = reaction::var(5);
    
    // 2. Build negated sum expression: -(a + b)
    auto negated_sum_expr = reaction::expr(- (var_a + var_b));
    
    // 3. Build logical NOT expression: !flag
    auto logical_not_expr = reaction::expr(!var_flag);
    
    // 4. Build bitwise NOT expression: ~num
    auto bitwise_not_expr = reaction::expr(~var_num);
    
    // 5. Get initial negated sum value
    int initial_negated_sum = negated_sum_expr.get();
    
    // 6. Update variables to trigger recomputation
    var_a.value(10);
    var_b.value(20);
    var_flag.value(false);
    var_num.value(3);
    
    // 7. Get updated values
    int updated_negated_sum = negated_sum_expr.get();
    bool logical_not_after_flip = logical_not_expr.get();
    int bitwise_not_value = bitwise_not_expr.get();
    
    // 8. Return unary expression snapshot
    return UnaryExpressionSnapshot{
        initial_negated_sum,
        updated_negated_sum,
        logical_not_after_flip,
        bitwise_not_value
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

This implementation follows all the specified requirements:

1. **Creates reactive variables** using `reaction::var()` for integers `a`, `b`, boolean flag, and integer `num`
2. **Builds negated sum expression** using `- (var_a + var_b)` with the unary `-` operator
3. **Creates reactive boolean variable** initialized to `true`
4. **Builds logical NOT expression** using `!var_flag` with the unary `!` operator
5. **Creates reactive integer variable** initialized to `5`
6. **Builds bitwise NOT expression** using `~var_num` with the unary `~` operator
7. **Gets initial negated sum value** using `.get()` method
8. **Updates variables** using `.value()` method to trigger recomputation
9. **Gets updated values** for all expressions
10. **Returns UnaryExpressionSnapshot** with the required field values

The implementation correctly uses the reactive programming paradigm where expressions automatically update when their dependencies change.</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/cache/cache_base.h>
#include <memory>
#include <chrono>
#include <functional>  // for std::hash and std::equal_to

namespace test_reaction {

struct GenericCacheStatsSnapshot {
    std::size_t entriesBefore;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInsert;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInvalidate;
    std::size_t hitCount;
    std::size_t missCount;
    double hitRatio;
    bool firstLookupWasMiss;
    bool secondLookupWasHit;
};

// Define the IntCache class
class IntCache : public reaction::CacheBase<int, int, std::hash<int>, std::equal_to<int>> {
public:
    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int, std::hash<int>, std::equal_to<int>>::CacheBase;

    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int, std::hash<int>, std::equal_to<int>>::getCachedValue;
    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int, std::hash<int>, std::equal_to<int>>::cacheValue;
    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int, std::hash<int>, std::equal_to<int>>::getStatsInternal;
    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int, std::hash<int>, std::equal_to<int>>::invalidateAllEntries;
    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int, std::hash<int>, std::equal_to<int>>::triggerCleanupInternal;
};

GenericCacheStatsSnapshot test_generic_cache_base() {
    constexpr static size_t MAX_SIZE = 100;
    constexpr static std::chrono::minutes TTL{60};

    // Step 2: Create IntCache instance
    IntCache cache;

    // Step 3: Get initial cache statistics
    auto initialStats = cache.getStatsInternal();

    // Step 4: Perform initial lookup (key 0)
    auto* firstResult = cache.getCachedValue(0);
    bool firstWasMiss = (firstResult == nullptr);

    // Step 5: Insert value into cache (key 0, value 42)
    cache.cacheValue(0, 42);

    // Step 6: Perform second lookup (key 0)
    auto* secondResult = cache.getCachedValue(0);
    bool secondWasHit = (secondResult != nullptr);

    // Step 7: Get cache statistics after insertion
    auto statsAfterInsert = cache.getStatsInternal();

    // Step 8: Invalidate all cache entries
    cache.invalidateAllEntries();

    // Step 9: Trigger cleanup
    cache.triggerCleanupInternal();

    // Step 10: Get cache statistics after invalidation
    auto statsAfterInvalidation = cache.getStatsInternal();

    // Step 11: Return the GenericCacheStatsSnapshot
    return GenericCacheStatsSnapshot{
        .entriesBefore = initialStats.totalEntries,
        .entriesAfterInsert = statsAfterInsert.totalEntries,
        .entriesAfterInvalidate = statsAfterInvalidation.totalEntries,
        .hitCount = statsAfterInsert.hitCount,
        .missCount = statsAfterInsert.missCount,
        .hitRatio = statsAfterInsert.hitRatio,
        .firstLookupWasMiss = firstWasMiss,
        .secondLookupWasHit = secondWasHit
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

instruction:
Please use functions from the `reaction` library to implement the `test_thread_manager_force_register` function.

The function should:
1. Get thread manager instance
2. Reset thread manager state
3. Check thread safety before registration
4. Register main thread
5. Check thread safety after main thread registration
6. Create worker thread and register it
7. Wait for worker thread to complete
8. Check thread safety after worker thread registration
9. Return force register snapshot

Please output the complete contents of the file:

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *
 * Collects observable state around calls to ThreadManager registration APIs.
 */
struct ForceRegisterSnapshot {
    bool safetyBeforeForce;
    bool safetyAfterMainForce;
    bool safetyAfterWorkerForce;
};

/**
 * Function: test_thread_manager_force_register
 *
 * Demonstrates how registering multiple distinct threads causes
 * ThreadManager to enable thread safety.
 *
 * - We start from a clean ThreadManager state.
 * - The main thread registers itself and remains the sole thread.
 * - A worker thread then also registers, which should trigger
 *   multi-thread detection and enable thread safety.
 *
 * @return ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *         A snapshot of the thread-safety flag before any registration,
 *         after the main-thread registration, and after the worker-thread registration.
 */
inline ForceRegisterSnapshot test_thread_manager_force_register();

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

The function should:

2. Build sum expression

Please output the complete contents of the file:

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <reaction/expression/expression_builders.h>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: RawExpressionSnapshot
 *
 * Captures the values produced by raw expression builder helpers
 * (`make_binary_expr`, `make_unary_expr`) before and after updating
 * their reactive inputs.
 */
struct RawExpressionSnapshot {
    int initialSum;
    int initialNegatedSum;
    int initialChainedValue;

    int updatedSum;
    int updatedNegatedSum;
    int updatedChainedValue;
};

/**
 * Function: test_raw_expression_builders
 *
 * Demonstrates how low-level expression builder helpers
 * (`make_binary_expr`, `make_unary_expr`) can be combined with
 * `expr` to build reactive calculations without using the higher
 * level `make_binary_op` / `make_unary_op` shortcuts.
 *
 * The function:
 * - Creates three reactive integers A, B, C.
 * - Builds sum = A + B via make_binary_expr.
 * - Builds negated = -(A + B) via make_unary_expr(make_binary_expr(...)).
 * - Builds chained = -(A + B) * C via nested make_* calls.
 * - Updates A and C and records all expression values before and after.
 *
 * @param a Initial value for the first input
 * @param b Initial value for the second input
 * @param c Initial value for the chaining multiplier
 * @return RawExpressionSnapshot
 *         Snapshot of sum / negated / chained values before and after updates.
 */
inline RawExpressionSnapshot test_raw_expression_builders(int a, int b, int c);

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/expression/atomic_operations.h>
#include <cstdint>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: AtomicHelperSnapshot
 *
 * Records the value of an integer after a sequence of atomic compound helper
 * operations and whether each operation reported a change.
 */
struct AtomicHelperSnapshot {
    int32_t finalValue;
    bool addReportedChange;
    bool multiplyReportedChange;
    bool andReportedChange;
    bool leftShiftReportedChange;
    bool incrementReportedChange;
};

/**
 * Helper implementation type that mimics the minimal interface required by
 * reaction::atomicCompoundAssign helpers. It stores an integer and exposes
 * an atomicOperation method that receives a lambda and tracks whether the
 * value was changed.
 */
class IntAtomicImpl {
public:
    explicit IntAtomicImpl(int32_t initial) : m_value(initial), m_lastReportedChange(false) {}
    
    template <typename F>
    void atomicOperation(F&& op) {
        atomicOperation(std::forward<F>(op), false);
    }
    
    template <typename F>
    void atomicOperation(F&& op, bool forceNotify) {
        bool changed = std::invoke(std::forward<F>(op), m_value);
        m_lastReportedChange = forceNotify ? true : changed;
    }
    
    int32_t value() const {
        return m_value;
    }
    
    bool lastReportedChange() const {
        return m_lastReportedChange;
    }

private:
    int32_t m_value;
    bool m_lastReportedChange;
};

/**
 * Function: test_atomic_compound_helpers
 *
 * Demonstrates the low-level atomic compound helper APIs:
 * - atomicCompoundAssign
 * - atomicAddAssign
 * - atomicMultiplyAssign
 * - atomicBitwiseAndAssign
 * - atomicLeftShiftAssign
 * - atomicIncrement
 *
 * These helpers operate on a simple IntAtomicImpl type instead of a full
 * React wrapper, making it easy to observe value and change reporting.
 *
 * @param initial Initial integer value
 * @return AtomicHelperSnapshot
 *         Snapshot containing the final value and which operations reported
 *         that a change occurred.
 */
inline AtomicHelperSnapshot test_atomic_compound_helpers(int32_t initial) {
    IntAtomicImpl atomic(initial);
    
    // Test atomicAddAssign
    reaction::atomicAddAssign(atomic, 5);
    bool addChanged = atomic.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Test atomicMultiplyAssign  
    reaction::atomicMultiplyAssign(atomic, 3);
    bool multiplyChanged = atomic.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Test atomicBitwiseAndAssign
    reaction::atomicBitwiseAndAssign(atomic, 0x0F);
    bool andChanged = atomic.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Test atomicLeftShiftAssign
    reaction::atomicLeftShiftAssign(atomic, 2);
    bool leftShiftChanged = atomic.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Test atomicIncrement
    reaction::atomicIncrement(atomic);
    bool incrementChanged = atomic.lastReportedChange();
    
    int32_t finalValue = atomic.value();
    
    return AtomicHelperSnapshot{
        .finalValue = finalValue,
        .addReportedChange = addChanged,
        .multiplyReportedChange = multiplyChanged,
        .andReportedChange = andChanged,
        .leftShiftReportedChange = leftShiftChanged,
        .incrementReportedChange = incrementChanged
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
